surface.name: Railway Quick Start Tutorial, with its recommended NextJS GitHub dashboard route as the complete candidate
surface.selection_basis: The Railway docs home leads with Quick Start and says it gets a first app running. The Quick Start covers GitHub, CLI, Docker image, and template routes. For a first code deployment, it recommends forking the prepared NextJS repository, labels GitHub Option 1, and explicitly says to choose Deploy Now for brevity. That documented recommendation makes the GitHub NextJS path a reconstructable candidate while preserving the peer route choices.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy with the Railway CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Quick Start lists this as Option 2 and requires CLI installation, a local clone, `railway init`, and `railway up`.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy from a Docker image
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Quick Start lists this as Option 3; private registry images require Pro.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy the Umami template
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The tutorial presents template deployment as a separate topic for preconfigured software, not the first listed bring-your-code route.

entry_point.developer_intent: Deploy a first web application to Railway and observe its running deployment.
assumed_prior_state: The developer can use a web browser and is permitted to accept Railway's Terms of Service.
The developer has or can create one Railway account. Railway permits one account per person and uses email, GitHub, and payment-method verification to enforce that rule.
The developer has a valid GitHub account and can fork the Railway-provided NextJS repository.
The trial account has enough remaining credit to build and run the first service.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Once the initial deployment is complete, your app is ready to go.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: Railway has built the forked NextJS source, started its container, and marked the long-running deployment Active on the Project Canvas.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The deployment view shows `Active`; without a configured healthcheck Railway assigns Active after starting the container, and the Quick Start says the app is ready once the initial deployment completes.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Once the initial deployment is complete, your app is ready to go.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: The Quick Start declares the app ready after initial deployment. Generating a public domain is conditional and follows that milestone, so a public URL is not required for this documented deployment-level first success.

prerequisites.requirement: The user must be at least 16 and accept Railway's Terms of Service.
One Railway account per person, created from the Login flow or already existing. The public docs do not reconstruct every browser signup screen.
A Free Trial, Free, Hobby, Pro, or Enterprise entitlement with enough credit and deployment capacity. A new trial grants $5 for up to 30 days before reverting to Free.
A valid GitHub account linked to Railway for the recommended GitHub route.
Permission to fork the official NextJS quickstart repository into the developer's GitHub account and authorize Railway to find it.

primary_path.action: Open Railway Documentation and select Quick Start.
primary_path.output: The Quick Start Tutorial opens as the docs-home path for getting a first app running.
primary_path.success_signal: The tutorial's GitHub, CLI, Docker image, and template routes are visible.

primary_path.action: Open Railway Login and choose Continue with GitHub or Log in using email. If no Railway account exists, complete the new-account continuation, whose atomic screens are not exposed by the public docs.
primary_path.input: A GitHub identity or email-based Railway authentication.
primary_path.output: An authenticated Railway account and dashboard session.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Users can have only one account. Email, GitHub, or payment-method verification may apply. The public docs do not enumerate the browser flow's atomic fields and ordering.

primary_path.action: For a newly created Free Trial account, assign a trial state and evaluate automated verification.
primary_path.input: The new Railway account and any connected GitHub account.
primary_path.output: A Full Trial or Limited Trial with a one-time $5 grant for up to 30 days.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An unverified Limited Trial still permits code deployment but restricts outbound network access and available ports. Railway does not respond to verification requests; upgrading to Hobby unlocks the full experience.

primary_path.action: Fork the basic NextJS repository linked by the Quick Start into the developer's GitHub account.
primary_path.input: The official Railway quickstart NextJS repository and a GitHub account.
primary_path.output: A fork of the prepared NextJS app is available in the developer's GitHub repositories.
primary_path.success_signal: The fork can be selected from Railway.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: GitHub controls the fork workflow; the Railway guide recommends this action but does not reproduce GitHub's atomic fork screens.

primary_path.action: Open the Railway dashboard and click New Project.

primary_path.output: Railway requests or uses a linked GitHub account and presents repository selection.
primary_path.success_signal: The GitHub repository search is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the Railway account is not associated with a valid GitHub account, Railway prompts the developer to link one.

primary_path.action: If prompted, link and authorize the valid GitHub account for Railway.
primary_path.output: Railway can list authorized GitHub repositories.
primary_path.success_signal: The forked NextJS repository appears in Railway search.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The Quick Start does not enumerate the current GitHub OAuth screens, repository scope choices, or intermediate confirmation states.

primary_path.action: Search for and select the forked NextJS repository.
primary_path.input: The repository name in Railway's GitHub project search.
primary_path.output: Railway presents Deploy Now and Add Variables choices.
primary_path.success_signal: The selected repository is ready for its first deployment decision.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A repository outside the authorized GitHub scope may not appear.

primary_path.action: Choose Deploy Now, as the tutorial directs for brevity.
primary_path.output: Railway creates a project, a service linked to the repository, and an initial deployment.
primary_path.success_signal: The Project Canvas opens and the first deployment begins.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing Add Variables instead opens the service to collect variables and requires a later click on Deploy at the top of the canvas.

primary_path.action: Accept the deployment into the queue and transition it from Initializing to Building.
primary_path.input: The NextJS repository source and default service configuration.
primary_path.output: Railway builds the code into a deployable container image using Railpack or a Dockerfile if present.
primary_path.success_signal: The deployment state advances from `Initializing` to `Building` and, after a successful build, to `Deploying`.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: For a Free-plan deployment during regional peak hours, Railway rejects the deploy until the developer waits outside the listed window or upgrades to Hobby or above. During separate limited-access periods, Free and sometimes Hobby deployments may queue; developers can wait or upgrade to Pro. A build error changes the deployment status to `Failed`.

primary_path.action: Start the built service using the detected or configured Start Command.
primary_path.success_signal: With no healthcheck, the deployment becomes `Active` after the container starts; with a healthcheck, Active waits for the healthcheck to succeed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A deploy error becomes `Failed`; a running deployment can later become `Crashed`. Build and deploy logs are available from the deployment menu.

primary_path.action: Verify the initial deployment on the Project Canvas and deployment view.
primary_path.output: The deployment's current state and logs are visible.
primary_path.success_signal: The deployment is `Active`; the Quick Start says the app is ready once the initial deployment completes.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If it fails, open build or deploy logs and review the entire log because the relevant error may not be at the bottom.

candidate_paths.name: Fork and deploy Railway's prepared NextJS app from GitHub
candidate_paths.selection_basis: The Quick Start explicitly recommends the prepared NextJS fork for first-time code deployment, lists GitHub first, and directs the reader to Deploy Now for brevity.
candidate_paths.relationship_to_primary_path: It makes the general Quick Start's first bring-your-code option executable while retaining CLI, Docker image, and template alternatives.

branches.condition: Railway cannot verify the new trial account through GitHub
branches.path: Continue on Limited Trial with restricted outbound networking and ports, or upgrade to Hobby for the full experience.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Code deployment remains permitted, but the deployed app's network behavior can differ.

branches.condition: The developer chooses CLI instead of the GitHub dashboard
branches.path: Install and authenticate the CLI, clone the NextJS fork, open a prompt in the local project, run `railway init`, provide the new project name, run `railway up`, then optionally `railway open` for the Project Canvas.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces GitHub dashboard source selection with local tooling, upload, and streamed logs.

branches.condition: The developer chooses the tutorial's Umami template
branches.path: Click New Project, Deploy a template, search for and select Umami, provide any requested information (none for the documented Umami example), and click Deploy.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provisions a multi-service preconfigured project rather than the developer's code.

branches.condition: The app needs environment variables before it can run
branches.path: Choose Add Variables, enter the required variables in the service, then click Deploy at the top of the canvas.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds configuration and a separate deploy action before the initial build.

branches.condition: The account is on the Free plan and the selected region is in its documented peak-hours window
branches.path: The deployment is rejected. Wait until outside the region's listed peak-hours window or upgrade to Hobby or above, then initiate the deployment again.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a time-window or plan-upgrade gate before Railway accepts the initial deployment.

branches.path: Wait for the healthcheck to succeed before Railway marks the deployment Active.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an application-level verification wait before the deployment terminal.

branches.condition: A public web URL is needed
branches.path: Open the service settings and click Generate Domain after the initial deployment is complete.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds public networking after the documented deployment-ready terminal.

friction_gates.description: The app path requires one Railway account per person; public docs do not reproduce the current browser signup sequence.
A user must accept Railway's Terms and meet the Terms' age requirement.
GitHub-based automated verification determines Full versus Limited Trial and Railway does not manually review verification requests.
Railway requires a linked valid GitHub account; the OAuth and repository-access sequence is external and not atomically documented in the Quick Start.
Deploy Now and Add Variables are peer actions. The tutorial chooses Deploy Now only for brevity.

The initial deploy moves through queue, build, and deploy states. Free-plan deploys are rejected during each region's peak-hours window, and Free or sometimes Hobby work may separately queue during limited-access periods.
A trial has $5 for up to 30 days, limits projects to five services, and limits RAM and vCPU. Exhausted credit can block or stop workloads.
For failure, inspect the build or deploy logs and read the full log. The deployment reference also provides Restart and Redeploy actions for relevant later states.

time_to_first_success.value: The docs home says Quick Start can deploy in minutes, but it does not state a numeric duration; Free-plan peak-hours rejection and separate Free or Hobby limited-access queues can extend the path.

Deploying your project - from a Docker image

sources.title: Deployments
evidence_supported: Initializing, Building, Deploying, Active, Failed and Crashed states

sources.title: Deployments
evidence_supported: Wait or Hobby-upgrade recovery for peak hours

sources.title: Login
evidence_supported: Current Log in using email option

uncertainties.question: What is the current browser signup, identity choice, and verification order?
uncertainties.impact: The number of account, legal, and verification steps before the dashboard cannot be counted precisely.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The public Login page exposes GitHub and email entry options, and official docs describe the one-account rule and verification classes, but they do not reproduce the new-account form fields or intermediate screens.

uncertainties.question: Does the prepared NextJS app currently require any project variables or a generated domain for the reader's intended success?
uncertainties.impact: A developer seeking visible browser output may need additional actions after Active deployment.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Quick Start directs Deploy Now for brevity and makes Generate Domain conditional, while it does not describe the app's rendered page as the required terminal.

excluded_after_success: Generating or attaching a public or custom domain.
Adding variables not required by the prepared NextJS sample.
Automatic GitHub redeploys after later pushes.
Databases, volumes, environments, teams, scaling, and observability configuration.
Production hardening, cost controls, rollback, and teardown.
